schools.summary: The positive turn — 'if ye then be risen with Christ' (assumed true, the other half of baptism's picture, 2:12; Rom 6) 'seek those things above, where Christ sitteth at God's right hand'; the duty flows from the privilege (Henry); against the earthbound rules of error, the nobler aim — not 'touch not, taste not' but 'seek the things above,' the heart following the risen Lord.

The inward disposition — 'set your affection/mind' (phroneite, keep on thinking about, cherishing) on things above, the habitual thoughts and loves fixed on heaven and Christ; not forbidding all thought of earthly matters or the lawful use of the present life (1 Cor 7:31), but forbidding their engrossing the heart (Calvin); the cure for earthbound living a mind set above.

themes: set your mind on things above

schools.summary: The ground of the heavenward mind — 'ye are dead' (died with Christ to sin and the world, Rom 6:2) and 'your life is hid with Christ in God' (kekryptai, perfect; the real spiritual life concealed, safely stored in Christ who is in God, beyond every enemy's reach — 'no hellish burglar can break that combination'); hiddenness not loss but safekeeping, awaiting glory.

themes: life hid with Christ in God

schools.summary: The glorious future — 'Christ, who is our life' (not merely the giver but our life itself, in whom it is hidden); 'when he shall appear' (phanerothe, the second coming, certain but undated); 'then shall ye also appear with him in glory' (the hidden life revealed, the saints displayed in their Lord's glory, 1 John 3:2); a choice consolation against present hiddenness (Calvin).

'Mortify' (nekrosate, put to death, treat as dead with a continued stroke) the 'members upon the earth' (not the limbs but the sinful passions working through them, the 'body of sin'); a catalogue from act to root — 'fornication, uncleanness' (impure deeds), 'inordinate affection, evil concupiscence' (disordered lusts), 'covetousness, which is idolatry' (greed giving created things the love due to God); the true mortification against ch2's false asceticism.

The motive of holy fear — the sins just named are no light matters (however lightly the heathen regarded them, Wesley); 'the wrath of God cometh' (Paul does not regard them as indifferent, Eph 5:6) on 'the children of disobedience'; the present tense pointing to wrath both now at work and to come (Calvin); the believer, no longer of the disobedient, must forsake what provokes such wrath.

Their former life — 'ye also walked' (periepatesate, the settled habit, outward acts) 'when ye lived in them' (ezete, imperfect, the inner life and element); living the inward principle, walking the outward acts (Wesley); these sins the very atmosphere of their heathen past; the reminder both humbling and urging them on, the break already made by grace.

The sins of temper and tongue — 'but now' (nyni de, the decisive change) 'put off' (apothesthe, strip off like filthy old clothes) 'anger' (settled feeling), 'wrath' (heated outburst), 'malice' (ill-will), 'blasphemy' (here slander/railing), 'filthy communication' (foul abusive speech); these spiritual sins of passion no less contrary to the gospel than the fleshly vices (Henry).

themes: the sins of temper and tongue

schools.summary: 'Lie not one to another' (me pseudesthe, stop lying / never begin), singled out for its destructiveness to fellowship; condemning not outright falsehood only but all cunning and deception, against sincerity (Calvin); the ground 'ye have put off the old man with his deeds' — lying belongs to the discarded self, unfitting for those who have stripped it off (cf Eph 4:25).

The new man put on (endysamenoi, worn as a garment, the fresh nature of regeneration, Gal 3:27), 'renewed in knowledge' (continually made new, esp. in true knowledge of God — not bare intellect but the Spirit's lively, effectual illumination, Calvin) 'after the image of him that created him' (the image of God lost in Adam restored in Christ); ignorance the mark of heathenism, knowledge of the Christian (Clarke).

Every human division abolished in the new man (ouk eni, does not exist; cf Gal 3:28) — 'neither Greek nor Jew' (national), 'circumcision nor uncircumcision' (religious, the false teachers' point), 'Barbarian, Scythian' (cultural, the Scythian the byword for savage), 'bond nor free' (social); 'but Christ is all, and in all' — Christ everything, dwelling in all the renewed alike, whatever their background.

themes: Christ is all and in all

schools.summary: The new garment of graces — 'as the elect of God, holy and beloved' (chosen, set apart, dearly loved, the motive and pattern); 'put on' (endysasthe) 'bowels of mercies' (heartfelt compassion), 'kindness,' 'humbleness of mind' (lowliness, opposed to ch2's false humility), 'meekness,' 'longsuffering'; the new man not only ceasing evil but learning to do well (Henry), these graces the evidences of renovation (Calvin).

'Forbearing one another' (anechomenoi, bearing patiently with faults, not retaliating) and 'forgiving one another' (charizomenoi, freely pardoning any grievance) — forbearance for present faults, forgiveness for past (Wesley); the pattern 'even as Christ forgave you' (his free, full pardon requiring no satisfaction, Clarke), the freely forgiven forgiving others — forgiveness a Christlike grace grounded in the gospel.

Love the crowning grace — 'above all these things put on charity' (worn over all the rest like the outer robe enveloping the clothing, Clarke), 'the bond of perfectness' (the ligament binding all the virtues into one harmonious, complete whole; without it they fall apart); love containing the whole of Christian perfection and harmonising all the special graces (Wesley, Ellicott).

'Let the peace of Christ' (the true reading, the peace he gives and is, John 14:27) 'rule' (brabeueto, act as umpire at the games, deciding and settling every inward conflict, governing the affections) 'in your hearts'; the corporate calling 'in one body' (the peace ruling each heart binding the church); 'be ye thankful' — gratitude accompanying peace, linking love of man with thankfulness to God (Ellicott).

'Let the word of Christ dwell in you' (take settled residence, not a passing visit) 'richly' (in the fullest measure, filling and governing the heart) 'in all wisdom'; the source of mutual ministry — 'teaching and admonishing one another' in 'psalms, hymns, spiritual songs' (sacred song a vehicle of teaching and worship), 'singing with grace in your hearts' (heart-worship springing from grace); against esoteric speculation, the plain word richly known and sung.

themes: the word of Christ dwell richly
singing with grace in the heart

schools.summary: The all-embracing rule — 'whatsoever ye do in word or deed' (everything without exception) 'do all in the name of the Lord Jesus' (by his authority, for his glory, as representing him), 'giving thanks to God by him' (through Christ the Mediator); the first principle of godly life, parallel to 'do all to the glory of God' (1 Cor 10:31); one comprehensive aim, not a multitude of petty rules.

themes: in the name of the Lord Jesus

schools.summary: The household code opens — 'wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands' (hypotassesthe, willing subjection to the husband's headship, the ordered yielding of the marriage relation, Eph 5:22); 'as it is fit in the Lord' (what is becoming in the Christian relationship, within the bounds of his will, not extending to what is contrary to him); not servile but the fitting order, transfigured by being 'in the Lord.'

themes: as it is fit in the Lord

schools.summary: The matching duty — 'love your wives' (present, 'keep on loving,' the steady habit of the heart, headship of love not domination); 'be not bitter' (me pikrainesthe, stop being harsh/resentful/exasperated, the husband's besetting sin, the corruption of authority; bitterness in word or spirit, Wesley); submission answered by love, each duty guarding the other from abuse.

'Children, obey your parents' (hypakouete, heeding, submitting obedience) 'in all things' (full and continual, not occasional, the hard part); bounded by God's will — obey in everything not contrary to his word, no command requiring sin (Clarke, Calvin); the motive 'well pleasing unto the Lord' (acceptable to God, a service to him, lifting the duty above mere family order).

'Provoke not your children to anger' (me erethizete, do not exasperate/nag as a habit, by harshness, continual fault-finding, or restless over-stimulation, Ellicott) 'lest they be discouraged' (athymosin, lose heart, give up, sink into despair or sullen stupidity, Wesley: desperate or stupid); harsh nagging breaks the spirit; authority to encourage, not crush.

Servants (slaves) — 'obey in all things your masters according to the flesh' (earthly lords, though Christ is their higher Lord) in all lawful things; 'not with eyeservice, as menpleasers' (ophthalmodouliais, the false diligence working only while watched); 'in singleness of heart' (sincere, single-minded honesty) 'fearing God' (from reverence for the God who sees all); the gospel dignifying the servant's lot, service to men done as service to God (Calvin).

The heart of all service — 'whatsoever ye do' (every task, echoing v17) 'do it heartily' (ek psyches, from the soul, cheerfully and diligently, not with outward show) 'as to the Lord, and not unto men' (rendered as service to Christ himself, the eye on him); the single-hearted never dejected by men's treatment, their aim higher (Wesley); the humblest task done for Christ becomes worship.

The servant's encouragement — 'of the Lord ye shall receive' (apolempsesthe, get back) 'the reward of the inheritance' (not a slave's wages but a son's portion; though owning nothing on earth, a heavenly inheritance stored up); 'for ye serve the Lord Christ' (the Christian servant's true Master Christ, who rewards the honest work done for the earthly lord as service to himself).

themes: of the Lord ye shall receive

schools.summary: The household code closes with impartial justice — 'he that doeth wrong shall receive' (komisetai) for it (repaid in kind by God); the wrongdoer unclear (master or slave) — Ellicott chiefly the master (Eph 6:9), Lightfoot both; 'no respect of persons' (God shows no partiality, judging all by one standard); comfort to the wronged servant and warning to the wronging master, undergirding the whole ethic.

debates.question: Is the 'wrongdoer' the master or the slave?

sides.position: chiefly the master, who is warned in the Ephesians parallel
both master and slave — the principle holds for either
